{"step_by_step_thinking": "The patient has a left C5 hemicord lesion, which is a spinal cord lesion affecting one half of the cord. A hemicord lesion at the C5 level is characteristic of Brown-Séquard syndrome. Brown-Séquard syndrome is defined by ipsilateral motor weakness and sensory loss (for pain and temperature), and contralateral loss of pain and temperature sensation, typically 1 to 3 levels above the level of injury. The contralateral loss of pain and temperature is due to involvement of the spinothalamic tract, which carries pain and temperature sensation and crosses in the spinal cord at the level of the decussation (around C1 to C4). However, the key feature of Brown-Séquard is the contralateral loss of pain and temperature, not motor paralysis. In this case, the patient has left-sided weakness, which is ipsilateral to the lesion, and the hemicord lesion implies involvement of the corticospinal tract on the left side. The corticospinal tract is responsible for motor function and decussates in the medulla, so the contralateral corticospinal tract is not directly involved. However, the lesion is at C5, which is in the cervical region, and the corticospinal tract decussates in the medulla, so involvement of the contralateral corticospinal tract is not expected. Instead, the ipsilateral motor weakness is due to the lesion in the corticospinal tract. Spastic paralysis at the level of the lesion is not typical in a hemicord lesion; rather, the paralysis is flaccid initially due to spinal shock and then becomes spastic. However, in Brown-Séquard, the paralysis is ipsilateral and spastic below the level of the lesion. Horner's syndrome (miosis, anhydrosis, ptosis) may occur if there is injury to the sympathetic chain, which is at the C7 to T1 level, not C5. Therefore, Horner's syndrome is not expected. Right-sided analgesia would be a finding of contralateral spinothalamic tract involvement, which is a hallmark of Brown-Séquard syndrome. The lesion at C5 would cause contralateral loss of pain and temperature on the right side. Therefore, the most likely finding is right-sided analgesia. Option D is correct.", "answer_choice": "D"}
